repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem

Neovim's `'autoread'` only detects external file changes reactively — on
`FocusGained`, `:checktime`, and similar events. This means changes made
by other editors, git operations, or build tools go unnoticed until the
user interacts with Neovim.

This has been a long-standing request (#1380) with two previous attempts:
- #12593 — introduced a new `fcnotify` option (closed: `'autoread'` should "just work")
- #20801 — simpler approach (closed: wanted to first establish `vim._watch` as shared infrastructure, which has since happened via the LSP watchfiles implementation)

## Solution

Add a runtime plugin (`runtime/plugin/autoread.lua`) that watches each
buffer's file using `vim._watch.watch()` (libuv `fs_event`). On change
detection, it calls `:checktime {bufnr}` which invokes the existing
`buf_check_timestamp()` logic — handling reload, conflict prompts,
`FileChangedShell` autocmd, etc.

Key details:
- Watches individual files (not directories) for targeted, low-overhead monitoring
- Debounces rapid events (e.g. truncate + write) with a 100ms timer
- Re-establishes the watcher after rename events (atomic saves by other editors)
- Respects buffer-local `:setlocal noautoread` and reacts to `OptionSet`
- Cleans up watchers on `BufUnload`/`BufDelete`/`BufWipeout`
- Auto-reloads are undoable


Closes #1380
Closes https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/7959

comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,142 @@
+--- Watches buffer files for external changes using vim._watch.
+--- When 'autoread' is set, external changes are detected in real-time
+--- instead of only on FocusGained/:checktime.
+
+local uv = vim.uv
+
+local group = vim.api.nvim_create_augroup('nvim.autoread', { clear = true })
+
+--- @type table<integer, fun()> bufnr -> cancel function
+local watchers = {}
+
+--- @type table<integer, uv.uv_timer_t> bufnr -> debounce timer
+local timers = {}
+
+local DEBOUNCE_MS = 100
+
+--- Returns the effective 'autoread' value for a buffer.
+--- 'autoread' is global-local: vim.bo[bufnr].autoread is nil when not set locally,
+--- so we must fall back to the global value.
+--- @param bufnr integer
+--- @return boolean
+local function buf_autoread(bufnr)
+  local local_val = vim.bo[bufnr].autoread
+  if local_val ~= nil then
+    return local_val
+  end
+  return vim.go.autoread
+end
+
+--- Returns true if the buffer should be watched.
+--- @param bufnr integer
+--- @return boolean
+local function should_watch(bufnr)
+  if not vim.api.nvim_buf_is_loaded(bufnr) then
+    return false
+  end
+  -- Skip special buffers (terminal, help, quickfix, etc.)
+  if vim.bo[bufnr].buftype ~= '' then
+    return false
+  end
+  -- Must have a file name that exists on disk
+  local name = vim.api.nvim_buf_get_name(bufnr)
+  if name == '' or not uv.fs_stat(name) then
+    return false
+  end
+  if not buf_autoread(bufnr) then
+    return false
+  end
+  return true
+end
+
+--- Stops and cleans up the watcher for a buffer.
+--- @param bufnr integer
+local function stop_watcher(bufnr)
+  local cancel = watchers[bufnr]
+  if cancel then
+    cancel()
+    watchers[bufnr] = nil
+  end
+  local timer = timers[bufnr]
+  if timer then
+    timer:stop()
+    timer:close()
+    timers[bufnr] = nil
+  end
+end
+
+--- Ensures the buffer has an active file watcher if appropriate, or stops
+--- an existing one if the buffer should no longer be watched.
+--- @param bufnr integer
+local function ensure_watcher(bufnr)

https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/1380#issuecomment-64139405 calls out : 

> the vim mechanism to save a file is overly complicated. If I edit the file with nano I just get one edit notification as expected, with vim I get, besides the edit notification, three(!) rename notification

does this PR avoid the redundant notifications? and does the test assert this?

comment_data.body: Which part specifically should live in `vim._watch`? The only really shareable part seems to be debouncing. It turns out, `_watch` module already declares `debounce` option in `Opts`, however it's only implemented for `watchdirs`, not for plain single-file `watch`. I've tried to implement it quickly - not exactly trivial, arranging it without much code duplication and avoiding any subtle behavior changes at the same time. I would suggest to push that improvement out of scope of this PR (but it would be a valid improvement nonetheless).

comment_data.body:
Turns that adding it as a core module and enabling it from `defaults.lua` comes with its own set of problems (`runtime` dir not available yet, would only work from VimEnter + affects tests that count exact number of autocmds). So, after observing CI failures, I've moved the registration to still happen from a "plugin" (just like other things we have there, like `matchparen`, `spellfile`, etc). The module itself remains a core module.

This would be an area for future improvement - make tests less fragile (count changes in autocmds, not exact numbers, etc). Out of scope for this PR.

comment_data.body:
From my perspective, disabling watchers on FocusLost kinda defeats the whole point of this change. Indeed, the obvious example - multiple tmux panes (or side-by-side terminal windows, etc) - user performs changes in one of the panes, would like to see the editor in the other pane reflect the up-to-date state of the file.

Even if we were to do this, any performance gains would be minimal in practice. It's not like we're constantly burning CPU in background in order to keep the watches. We'll only wake up when there is an actual change.

Therefore, my recommendation - no, don't. Keep the behavior as written, keep the watches always on, unless user opts-out (and you already described how to do the opt-out).

comment_data.body:
this is another weak test. it's probably worth adding a private function like `autoread._is_watching(bufnr: integer)` so that tests can make a stronger assertion here.

and then that 1-line assertion can be in every test, instead of a single `cleans up watcher on bdelete` test (which is no longer needed). that's an example of what i meant by "avoid over-granular tests".

also to further strengthen tests, they should check both `_is_watching == true` and `_is_watching == false` at the right times.

comment_regular_data.body: Can you please pull out the `fix` into a separate PR? It shouldn't be blocked by what is sure to be a long discussion on the `feat` (e.g., if it's a plugin, it should probably be opt-in at least at first), which also prevents simple backporting.

> Can you please pull out the `fix` into a separate PR? It shouldn't be blocked by what is sure to be a long discussion on the `feat` (e.g., if it's a plugin, it should probably be opt-in at least at first), which also prevents simple backporting.

Extracted into its own PR, as requested, here: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/37973

I remembered about this change and merged it into my working branch wanting to see changes on single buffer made by grug-far.nvim. 

It would be nice to have a mention of how to turn it off (or even prevent autocommands from being created in the current solution) or on (if switched to opt-in).

This has been sitting here for quite a while, with no feedback from maintainers. Yet the feature is still relevant.
@zeertzjq, @justinmk - any comments on this approach? It would be great to have this in `neovim` upstream.

> This has been sitting here for quite a while, with no feedback from maintainers

Because it's high risk. And there's a lot going on (we are merging 500+ PRs per month).

It's good that you noticed `vim._watch` and are attempting to leverage that. And the "Key details" sound very good.

 It would increase confidence a lot more if (1) you keep this rebased regularly, (2) you do your own review and call out key decisions, and how they fit into the codebase conventions.

There are at least 2 very painful LSP-related "file watcher" bugs that no one seems to want to spend time looking into: 

- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/29865
- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/23291

So I need confidence that we aren't going to have more of that with 'autoread'. And yes, this is a high-value and desired change, but we need an owner for it who will followup.

I've rebased and addressed outstanding comments. @justinmk, please take another look.

The two linked example LSP/watch related bugs - we shouldn't expect any trouble like that from here. This autoread implementation only watches individual files, specifically (so, no globs, no recursive potentially expensive directory watches, etc). Designed to be very straightforward / obvious, i.e. "for every file open in the editor, establish a targeted file watch, invoke already existing checktime() to reload, clean up watches on file close / exit".
please check if any of the test can be reasonably combined; avoid over-granular `it()` blocks.

> please check if any of the test can be reasonably combined; avoid over-granular `it()` blocks.

So I've removed the one test of questionable value, and combined two that dealt with `autoread` option changes. But the rest seem like genuinely separate cases that we'd like to test. So not sure what's the benefit of combining them further? That goes against the normal best practices for unit tests. If you have specific ones in mind that you think we should combine, please name them explicitly.

Overall, the branch has been rebased and the outstanding comments addressed. @justinmk - please take another look.
Comments addressed, ready for another review round, @justinmk
